# Justin Tan & Watjana Lilaonitkul (Digital Health and AI Technologies)

<!-- vimeo: 1111497120 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

Hello, my name is Justin Tan and I lecture postgraduate students at GBSH, the Global Business School for Health at University College London. Picture a class of healthcare technology and business professionals at different stages of their careers together, the smattering of fresh grads all stereo expectingly at new on day one and in 10 weeks. The goal is to have everyone in this disparate group build a working digital health prototype based on a real healthcare problem they identify. Thanks, Justin. Hi, I'm WA Lila Wingo at UCLA's Global Business School for Health. In our 10 week digital health and AI technologies module, students use AI on real health problems, not just learn about it. We build judgment first, then create responsibly. We also use chat GBT in a human LED proposed check refined loop with curated sources, no personal data and human review. It's SPED design without writing the course. Classes are flipped for application two formats drive it. Interactive case studies where better questions unlock new exhibits and A WHO style simulation where students debate trade-offs toward a shared framework. In parallel, practicals run the capstone here, mixed teams build a working GPT assistant for a chosen problem. The aim throughout is more realism, faster iteration, and broader participation within the same teaching. Time over to Justin for the first format. So a big part of our course senses on highly interactive structured case studies designed from the ground up. With the help of chat GBT in one of these case studies, we asked our students working in teams to play the role of consultants advising a hospital on the implementation of an AI powered emergency department tri system. Rather than dumping a conventional case spec on them, we thought, why not work with chat GPT to design something more innovative? And we landed on what we're calling a progressive locked mechanism. So in practical terms, this means that all teams begin with the same briefing note about the hospital and the challenge they face. However, each insightful question they post will review a new exhibit. This could be a financial model, staffing rosters, technology assessment report, or perhaps clinician interviews. As a result of having access to different types of information, making different decisions, each team will land at a different endpoint, which makes for a much richer learning experience when we bring everyone back together. The approach we took to develop this exercise was to first share the learning objectives with chat GPT. Then brainstorm and debate several options for the case study structure, which reiterated chat. GPT also helped with researching real life examples, which we used to inform the case study structure, and was also instrumental in drafting the exhibits student and facilitator handouts and conducting overall stress testing of the exercise. Now, while we could have bought a pre-made case pack, what we created with chat GBT was designed specifically for context and curriculum. And we believe that this makes our case study far more relevant, far more effective than anything that we could have purchased off the shelf. Next, the WHO simulation, a fictional summit on cross-border AI and data use built for experiential learning. Students had just enough structure to work independently and negotiate in groups real time. Each student took a delicate role from ministries, regulators, funders, industry clinicians, or patient advocates across high, middle, and low resource settings. Tables have natural allies and built in tensions, so teams had to find common ground and choose compromises and defend priorities. The teaching team designed the simulation chat. GPT was a sparring partner under our direction. It helped us balance the cast spot contradictions between the delegate briefs and the shared scenario and stress test edge cases with what if prompts. It also helped tighten wording to one page per brief and kept scope safe and on topic within our guardrails. Within this operative loop, we made the call and we wrote where needed. Students then worked from the one page briefs with context, objectives, constraints, and a short evidence pack. Then the room moved fast from opening statements to quick caucuses and negotiations on accountability, transparency, and patient protection. By the end, every table had a concrete framework to vote on. Then we debriefed back to privacy, access, equity, and algorithmic accountability. The result was a genuine shift in students' views beyond their starting positions with educators. In the lead chat, GPT sped, the design helped surface blind spots and created a space to shape a richer human learning experience. For the capstone students did not just talk about ai, they built it using the open AI custom GPT builder. Each team created a working GPT for a real healthcare problem. Not a paper exercise, but a usable prototype. Their workflow was tight. Scope a real problem, define success, curate a focus knowledge base, write clear system instructions, and shape the conversation for actionable outputs. Then test, refine, repeat. They pressure tested with role play using personas co-designed with chat GPT. For example, a time pressed clinician, a user with low digital literacy, a multilingual user. They added constraints to check inclusivity under stress guardrails, kept it safe and focused. No personal data, classroom prototypes only, and evidence LED pivots when a path looked risky. So what worked? Keeping it small and curated to cut drift defining, done early. So iterations, target measurable outcomes, and iterate fast. With chat GBT removing coding frictions, time went to the problem. The evidence and the evaluation impact was clear. Every team delivered in one term. Examples include revoice, a gamified stroke rehab coach Elias, a clinician support tool and climate bot, a disaster zone first aid helper. Most important students finish with sharper habits, better questions, faster evidence use, and stronger cross-disciplinary collaboration. The through line was simple judgment first, then build, build it safe, focused, and real enough to matter. So what's been the broader impact of integrating chat GBT into our course? Firstly, efficiency and impact without having used chat. GPT achieving this level of fidelity and contextual nuance for the course would frankly have been unaffordable in both time and effort. Secondly, equity. Every student can now experience shipping a working prototype regardless of whether they arrive in the course as a coder or clinician. And finally, engagement. We received overwhelmingly positive student feedback for the course with many noting how deeply developed the case studies and simulations were, and several even commented they had never before experienced such immersive and realistic learning scenarios. In a university course going forward, we intend to deepen our exploration into human AI collaboration in the context of education. This could include, for instance, employing Socratic methods such as those available with chat GBT study mode, or potentially flipping the script and employing dialectic role reversal where we have students instead teach concepts back to check GPT to sharpen their own understanding. We're also intrigued by the potential to leverage AI to personalize our curriculum more deeply aligning educational delivery, whether in the form of video, text, audio, or dialogues. With the diverse learning preferences of our students, We believe generative AI can play a lasting role in the classroom when it's used with care and purpose. In this course, we kept a human led loop and clear guardrails. We used tools like chat GBT to help design learning experiences and to help teams build working assistance. The payoff was more realism, faster iteration, and broader participation all within the same teaching time. If you are exploring similar approaches, we'd love to exchange notes and share materials. Drop us a line. Let's co-create the next wave of learning experiences.
